lead: Moving day in Woodbridge demands a little more forethought than most, and that is no bad thing. The town's narrow historic streets, period homes and tight parking reward the families who prepare, and after two decades of Woodbridge moves from our Westerfield base we've learned exactly which jobs make the difference. This checklist brings together everything we'd want a Woodbridge customer to know, from arranging access on a cramped lane to the final sweep of an old cottage, so your move-in day runs smoothly.

sections.h: The Week Before: Build In the Buffer
sections.body:
A Woodbridge move runs far better when the week before it is used to build in some breathing room. By now your packing should be nearly finished, with only the kitchen and bathroom essentials left for the final morning, and every box labelled on the side as well as the top with its room and contents. This matters all the more in a period home, where carrying boxes up and down a steep, narrow staircase is part of the day, and clear labels keep the flow orderly rather than chaotic. Check the loft, shed and any outbuildings now, not on the morning.

Use the week to confirm the practical details too. Pin down the crew's arrival time, ensure someone will be at both properties, and sort out the keys for handover. Defrost the freezer a couple of days ahead so it travels dry, run down the fridge, and arrange care for pets and young children on the day if you can, since an empty house is both safer and quicker to load through tight doorways.

Finally, pack an essentials box that travels with you in the car rather than the van: kettle, mugs, tea, phone chargers, snacks, toilet roll, medicines and a change of clothes. After a day spent moving into a characterful old house, you will be grateful not to be hunting through a wall of boxes for the basics on your first evening in Woodbridge.

Access is the defining challenge of a Woodbridge move, and it is worth real attention. Many of the older streets in the historic core were never built for large vehicles, and parking close to a period property can be genuinely difficult. If your home sits on one of these tight lanes, tell us early. We can plan smaller shuttle loads to ferry your belongings from a lorry parked nearby, and where needed you may be able to arrange a temporary parking suspension with the council so we can get closer to the door.

Riverside addresses near the quayside and the Tide Mill carry their own quirks, with narrow approaches and limited room to manoeuvre, so a quick photo of the parking and access at both your old and new homes sent over when you book lets us bring the right vehicle and the right number of crew. The more we know in advance, the less there is to improvise on the day.

A friendly word to neighbours that a removal lorry will need space outside for a few hours goes a long way on a cramped Woodbridge street. And if you have your own car, move it well clear of your access the night before. Away from the historic centre, on the more modern roads, access is usually much easier, but even there a little forethought about the kerb outside keeps loading and unloading running without hold-ups.

sections.h: Moving Morning in an Old House
sections.body:
When the crew arrives, take a few minutes to walk us through the house, which is especially important in an older property full of original features. Point out anything fragile or precious, flag any items not coming with you so nothing is loaded by mistake, and make sure walkways are clear and rugs rolled back. On a steep period staircase or through a low doorway, a clear route is what lets us move heavy furniture safely and without scuffing the very features that make the house special.

Keep the kettle and a few mugs to hand until the last moment, as a brew is always welcome on a busy morning. Settle children and pets somewhere safe and out from underfoot so the team can work freely. We protect floors, banisters and doorways as we go, taking particular care in a characterful home, but an uncluttered space is the best protection of all and keeps the loading moving steadily.

As the van fills, keep a running tally of the larger items, including anything from the garden, the shed or a summerhouse, which are easy to overlook. It is far simpler to notice that something has not been loaded while we are still on site than to discover it once we have pulled away down a narrow lane. Pause before you lock up and let yourself register that the hardest part is already behind you.

sections.h: The Route and the Final Sweep
sections.body:
Most Woodbridge moves involve a short journey, but the route through and out of the town still needs thought given the narrow streets, and if your move takes you further afield we plan timings around the roads towards Ipswich to avoid sitting in traffic with your belongings aboard. If you are following in your own car, there is no need to keep us in sight; simply head to the new place and we will meet you there. We choose the right vehicle for the lanes involved at both ends.

Before you leave the old house for the last time, do a thorough final sweep with us. Check every cupboard, behind every door, the loft, the garden, the shed and any riverside store, because it is remarkable what gets left behind in the last rush, particularly in a rambling old property with plenty of nooks. Take and photograph your meter readings, lock every window, and make sure you have the keys for the new home and a clear plan for handing over the old ones.

Leaving the house clean and the rubbish dealt with is a courtesy to whoever follows and a good way to part on friendly terms with neighbours you may still see around town. Once the door is locked behind you, the old chapter is closed and your Woodbridge future begins, just along the road by the Deben.

sections.h: Arriving and Unloading at the New Home
sections.body:
At the new Woodbridge house, the careful labelling pays off handsomely. As we carry each box in we place it in its room according to the label, turning the days ahead from a treasure hunt into a tidy tick-list, which is doubly welcome when the staircase is steep and you would rather not carry anything twice. Position yourself where you can direct the flow, pointing the crew towards the right rooms and confirming where the larger furniture should go before it is set down.

Check your inventory against what arrives, especially if anything travelled separately or came out of storage. We will reassemble the beds and wardrobes we took apart at the old house, so your first night in Woodbridge is a comfortable one rather than a camp-out among the boxes. Make the beds, find the kettle, and resist the urge to unpack the whole house at once; focus on the rooms you need first and let the rest wait until morning.

Once the van is empty and you have confirmed nothing is left aboard, the move is done. Locate the stopcock and the fuse board, test the heating, and then take a moment, perhaps a short walk down to the quayside, to mark your arrival. A well-run Woodbridge move should leave you tired but settled, ready to enjoy the town rather than buried beneath a mountain of unopened boxes.

Not every Woodbridge move runs neatly from one front door to another in a single day. Chains slip, completion dates move, and sometimes the new place is not ready when you have to leave the old one. This is where secure storage quietly eases the move. We've helped many Woodbridge families bridge an awkward gap of days or weeks by keeping their belongings safe and delivering when the new home is finally theirs, taking the strain off an uncertain chain.

If there is any chance you might need it, raise storage when you first get in touch rather than at the last minute. That lets us plan the whole move as one smooth operation, packing and labelling everything as if it were a normal move and setting aside what you will need to reach in the meantime. It is far calmer than trying to force an unsettled move into one hectic day, which is no fun on a town's narrow streets.

Storage also suits the gentler reasons that often surround a Woodbridge move: downsizing from a larger family home and needing somewhere for furniture while you decide, renovating a period property before you fully move in, or simply wanting breathing space. In every case, a secure place to keep your things means you move into your new Woodbridge home properly and on your own terms, which after twenty years of doing this is exactly the outcome we want.

faqs: How do I arrange access for a removal lorry on a narrow Woodbridge street?

Many historic Woodbridge streets weren't built for large vehicles, so you may be able to arrange a temporary parking suspension with the council, and where a lorry can't get close we plan smaller shuttle loads. Send us a photo of the access at both ends when you book so we bring the right vehicle.
Will my furniture fit through the doors of an old Woodbridge house?

Usually, with care. Period homes here have low doorways, tight hallways and steep stairs, so we dismantle bulky furniture where needed and protect original features as we carry it through. Tell us about any large or awkward items when we survey so we can plan ahead.
What should I keep in the essentials box for a Woodbridge move?

Pack a box that travels in your car with the kettle, mugs, tea, phone chargers, snacks, toilet roll, medicines and a change of clothes. After a day moving into a characterful Woodbridge home, you'll be glad not to hunt through boxes for the basics that evening.
Can you store our belongings if the Woodbridge completion is delayed?

Yes. Our secure storage is ideal when a chain slips or the new home isn't ready. We keep everything safe until your new Woodbridge home is ready and deliver when you are. Mention it when you book so we plan the whole move as one smooth operation.
